title: 1974: A Computer on Your Desk
lesson:
The time machine lands in a garage. In front of you is the Altair 8800, a blue box covered in toggle switches and red LEDs, built around Intel's 8080 microprocessor. In January 1975 it landed on the cover of Popular Electronics as a $439 mail-order kit, and it changed everything: for the first time, an ordinary person could own a computer. Thousands sold in months. Two young programmers named Bill Gates and Paul Allen wrote a BASIC interpreter for it and started a company called Microsoft. The personal-computer era begins right here.

The 8080 is a real leap in registers. The PDP-8 had one accumulator, but the 8080 has the accumulator A plus six more general registers: B, C, D, E, H, and L. More registers means you can keep more values close to the CPU and shuffle data around without constantly going back to memory. MVI loads a register with an immediate value, MOV copies between registers, and ADD adds a register into the accumulator A.
prompt: Put 5 in register A and 7 in register B, then add B into A so A ends up at 12. Finish with HLT.
starterCode:
MVI A, 5    ; A = 5
        MVI B, 7    ; B = 7
        ADD B       ; A = A + B
        HLT
hints: MVI A, 5 loads the value 5 into register A.
ADD B adds register B into the accumulator A.
successText: Register A must equal 12 (that's 5 plus 7) at the halt.
reference:
MVI A, 5
MVI B, 7
ADD B
HLT
